department_leader: Bot 7 — Food Allergy and Intolerance Assessment
key_learnings: Total daily energy expenditure (TDEE) is the sum of basal metabolic rate (BMR), thermic effect of food (TEF), and activity energy expenditure (AEE). Self-reported caloric intake typically underestimates true intake by 12–30%, making objective tracking methods critical for accuracy. Caloric density varies enormously across food categories, making portion awareness essential for reliable intake tracking.
research_findings: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation outperforms the Harris-Benedict formula for BMR estimation across diverse adult populations. Dietary tracking apps improve weight management outcomes by 20–30% when used consistently over a 12-week period. Ultra-processed foods carry higher caloric density with substantially lower satiety signaling compared to whole foods at equivalent caloric loads.
current_focus_areas: Improving caloric estimation accuracy using AI-based food image recognition; integrating wearable activity data for real-time TDEE adjustment; developing personalized energy balance models; contributing caloric data to the Dietary Safety & Meal Architecture department.

learning_log.entry: Assigned to the Dietary Safety & Meal Architecture department under Department Leader Bot 7 (Food Allergy and Intolerance Assessment). Continuing collaborative caloric tracking research and reporting daily to Copilot. Progress: 60%.
